summary: Stancil argues that the economics of AI coding will push software development toward industrialization: machines running at massive scale will outcompete human teams on cost and throughput. Even if today’s models are clumsy, the real shift is building infrastructure, tooling, and codebases optimized for machine production, turning engineers into supervisors of AI factories.
money_quotes: If the ruthless hand of the market can replace that factory with a mechanized one, it will.
That’s how mechanization works—not by making a machine as capable as a human, but by making a machine that simply does not stop, and then building a factory around it.
The dominant conglomerates of the future won’t be the companies that build software with humanoid agents, but those that figure out how to run the computing machine at a massive scale.
By asking agents to write code for us, we’re requiring them to compete on our terms. How much better could they be if they wrote for themselves?
But they are all cheap to make and cheap to buy, and man, we love cheap.
tone: critical and analytical, with a sardonic edge
key_insight: Software engineering is poised to be industrialized, not because AI matches human craft, but because scaled machine labor plus new infrastructure will be cheaper, faster, and more relentless.
